From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International online pop culture magazine

*PopMatters*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Managing Editor | Karen Zarker |
| Founder | Sarah Zupko |
| Founded | October 1999; 26 years ago (1999-10) |
| Based in | Chicago |
| Language | English |
| Website | [popmatters.com](https://popmatters.com) |
| [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") | [1122752384](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1122752384) |

***PopMatters*** is an international [online magazine](/wiki/Online_magazine "Online magazine") of cultural criticism that covers aspects of [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"). *PopMatters* publishes reviews, interviews, and essays on cultural products and expressions in areas such as [music](/wiki/Music "Music"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [television](/wiki/Television "Television"), [films](/wiki/Film "Film"), [books](/wiki/Book "Book"), [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"), [comics](/wiki/Comics "Comics"), [sports](/wiki/Sports "Sports"), [theater](/wiki/Theater "Theater"), [visual arts](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts"), [travel](/wiki/Travel "Travel"), and the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet").[[2]](#cite_note-popmatters-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=PopMatters&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

*PopMatters* was founded by Sarah Zupko, who had previously established the cultural studies academic resource site PopCultures.[[3]](#cite_note-3) *PopMatters* launched in late 1999[[4]](#cite_note-4) as a sister site providing original essays, reviews and criticism of various media products. Over time, the site went from a weekly publication schedule to a five-day-a-week magazine format, expanding into regular reviews, features, and columns. In the fall of 2005, monthly readership exceeded one million readers.

From 2006 onward, *PopMatters* produced several syndicated newspaper columns for [McClatchy-Tribune News Service](/wiki/McClatchy-Tribune_News_Service "McClatchy-Tribune News Service").[[5]](#cite_note-5) By 2009 there were four different pop culture related columns each week.

The PopMatters Book Imprint published *Joss Whedon: The Complete Companion*, edited by Mary Money, with [Titan Books](/wiki/Titan_Books "Titan Books") in May 2012. The imprint also published four books in a series with [Counterpoint](/wiki/Counterpoint_(publisher) "Counterpoint (publisher)")/[Soft Skull](/wiki/Soft_Skull "Soft Skull")[[6]](#cite_note-6) in 2008–2009 including *China Underground* by Zachary Mexico, *Apocalypse Jukebox: The End of the World in American Popular Music* by Edward Whitelock and David Janssen, *Rebels Wit Attitude: Subversive Rock Humorists* by Iain Ellis, and *The Solitary Vice: Against Reading* by [Mikita Brottman](/wiki/Mikita_Brottman "Mikita Brottman").

## Staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=PopMatters&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Staff")]

*PopMatters* publishes content from worldwide contributors. Its staff includes writers from backgrounds ranging from academics and professional journalists to career professionals and first time writers. Many of its writers are published authorities in various fields of study.[[2]](#cite_note-popmatters-2)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Notable contributors include [David Weigel](/wiki/David_Weigel "David Weigel"), political reporter for *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")*,[[8]](#cite_note-David_Weigel-8) [Steven Hyden](/wiki/Steven_Hyden "Steven Hyden"), staff writer for *[Grantland](/wiki/Grantland "Grantland")* and author of *Whatever Happened to Alternative Nation?*,[[9]](#cite_note-9) writer and author J.C. Maçek III,[[10]](#cite_note-KR-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) [documentarian](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") and writer [Rodger Jacobs](/wiki/Rodger_Jacobs "Rodger Jacobs")[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) and Rob Horning, executive editor of *The New Inquiry*.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Karen Zarker is the managing editor.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
